title: Jesus Simulator
anchor_summary: A narrative-driven historical/religious story game presented largely as a visual novel, emphasizing dialogue, shifting viewpoints, key story events, and light interactive mini-games rather than deep action systems.

similar_games.title: Pentiment
similar_games.why_similar: Dialogue-led historical narrative adventure focused on character perspectives, choice-driven scenes, and authentic period storytelling.

similar_games.title: Attentat 1942
similar_games.why_similar: Historically grounded interactive narrative with educational intent, conversational progression, and simple mini-game segments.

similar_games.title: 1979 Revolution: Black Friday
similar_games.why_similar: Story-first historical retelling built around witnessing major events, making dialogue choices, and moving through key set-piece moments.

similar_games.title: Valiant Hearts: The Great War
similar_games.why_similar: Respectful historical storytelling delivered through episodic scenes and light gameplay interactions rather than combat-heavy systems.

similar_games.title: The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante
similar_games.why_similar: Text-forward narrative experience centered on morality, faith, and life-path choices across a structured story arc.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Jesus Simulator as a 'narrative visual novel experience' that retells the Gospel through dialogue, key story moments, multiple viewpoints, and interactive mini-games. The text emphasizes authored historical narrative, spiritual reflection, and witnessing events rather than combat, systems-driven simulation, or open-ended RPG play. Single-player mode is directly supported by store metadata. Although one metadata label mentions 'First-Person Adventure' and generic RPG/simulation tags appear, the detailed gameplay copy is much more specific and strongly supports visual_novel as the core identity.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Dialogue-led historical narrative adventure focused on character perspectives, choice-driven scenes, and authentic period storytelling.

title: Moto Rush Reborn
anchor_summary: Level-based arcade motorcycle racing with strong endless-runner reflex demands: lane-splitting through traffic, duck/slide/jump obstacle avoidance, score/time objectives, and replay-driven mastery.

similar_games.title: Traffic Rider
similar_games.why_similar: First-person motorcycle traffic-dodging built around speed, near misses, and weaving through busy roads.

similar_games.title: Distance
similar_games.why_similar: High-speed obstacle-course driving focused on jumping, dodging hazards, precision movement, and replaying levels for mastery.

similar_games.title: Trials Rising
similar_games.why_similar: Motorcycle-based, level-structured skill game emphasizing obstacle navigation, stunt inputs, and repeated runs for better performance.

similar_games.title: Road Redemption
similar_games.why_similar: Arcade motorcycle racing through traffic and hazardous roads, though it leans more heavily on combat and roguelite progression.

similar_games.title: Neon Drive
similar_games.why_similar: Reflex-heavy lane-switching and obstacle avoidance at speed in a stylized neon driving format closer to a runner than a sim racer.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify a level-based motorcycle racing game centered on clearing fast-paced tracks, weaving through traffic, avoiding obstacles, chasing record times, and completing skill objectives. The core loop is explicitly vehicular racing with vehicle control, speed management, track hazards, unlockable levels, and score/time-performance goals rather than simulation realism. The copy also describes 45 levels, collectible symbols, and chapter unlocks, supporting an arcade-style campaign structure with replayable stage mastery. '3D runner' phrasing adds obstacle-dodging flavor, but the dominant identity remains arcade racing.

title: RUBATO

similar_games.title: Yoku's Island Express
similar_games.why_similar: 2D exploration-platforming built around physics interactions, collectible progression, and interconnected metroidvania-like areas.

similar_games.title: Wuppo
similar_games.why_similar: Offbeat 2D action-adventure with humorous writing, side-scrolling exploration, questing, and compact world discovery.

similar_games.title: Tomba!
similar_games.why_similar: 2D platform-adventure with semi-open stages, objective-driven progression, item collection, and a playful character-action feel.

similar_games.title: Ori and the Blind Forest
similar_games.why_similar: Side-scrolling platform exploration through interconnected zones with ability-gated progression and collectible-focused advancement.

similar_games.title: Cavern of Dreams
similar_games.why_similar: Modern collectathon centered on dense exploration, secret hunting, and compact worlds, even if its perspective is 3D rather than side-scrolling.

payload.anchor_summary: A quirky 2D side-scrolling collectathon-platformer with semi-open/metroidvania-like areas, physics-driven traversal interactions, dense collectible hunting, and a strongly authored humorous adventure tone.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official text is sufficient and consistent. Steam explicitly calls RUBATO an 'experimental, 2D physics-based take on the collect-a-thon genre' and says players move through 'open metroidvania-like areas' across '5 compact, densely packed areas' while uncovering 'secrets and shortcuts in every corner of the world.' That strongly supports a side-scrolling exploration-platformer structure with semi-open interconnected spaces and discovery gating, which fits metroidvania better than the 3d_collectathon archetype because the game is explicitly 2D. The description also mentions interacting with enemies and objects ('use your tongue to lick enemies and objects') and collecting Planet Bits to fix the solar system, supporting moderate action layered onto exploration. Narrative framing is authored and character-focused, but gameplay identity is primarily side-scrolling exploratory platforming.

title: Inky Blinky Bob
anchor_summary: Vehicle-centric survival action built around piloting and upgrading a mobile craft, scavenging resources from dangerous locations, and mixing mounted combat with on-foot fights while progressing across a hostile world.

similar_games.title: Forever Skies
similar_games.why_similar: First-person survival centered on a customizable airship, scavenging ruined locations, crafting upgrades, and staying mobile in a hostile world.

similar_games.title: Volcanoids
similar_games.why_similar: Survival crafting with a weaponized mobile base at the core of traversal, resource runs, repairs, and combat against environmental threats and enemies.

similar_games.title: Raft
similar_games.why_similar: Progression is driven by upgrading your moving base, scavenging materials, visiting islands, and balancing exploration with survival needs.

similar_games.title: Voidtrain
similar_games.why_similar: A moving vehicle serves as both transport and upgrade platform while you gather resources, craft improvements, and fend off threats between destinations.

similar_games.title: FAR: Changing Tides
similar_games.why_similar: Vehicle-first adventure where maintaining and piloting your craft is the main loop, though it is lighter on combat and survival systems.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently describe a single-player action-horror campaign built around piloting a weaponized hot air balloon across a chain of islands, scavenging resources, repairing and upgrading the vehicle, managing limited med kits, and fighting failed experiments with mounted weapons plus close-range axe/shotgun combat. The game emphasizes survival pressure, hostile horror theming, freely flying between islands, and objective/gig-based progression toward powering towers and reaching a sealed facility. This strongly supports action_horror as the best fit, with survival-adventure and open-world action-adventure traits as secondary influences. Perspective and exact camera are not stated, so those fields are left empty.

title: Ghost Master: Resurrection
anchor_summary: A faithful remake of Ghost Master built around indirect-control strategy: deploy and coordinate ghosts across scenario-based maps, manage supernatural resources, frighten humans through ability synergies, and solve puzzle-like objectives in a spooky management shell.

similar_games.title: Ghost Master
similar_games.why_similar: The original game this remake is based on; same core loop of placing specters, chaining haunt abilities, and solving scenario objectives through indirect control.

similar_games.title: Haunt the House: Terrortown
similar_games.why_similar: Another game about possessing objects and escalating fear to drive humans out, emphasizing playful haunting and indirect scare-based puzzle play.

similar_games.title: MachiaVillain
similar_games.why_similar: Monster-management strategy with room setup, creature abilities, and orchestrating horror outcomes, sharing the macabre management and planning-heavy feel.

similar_games.title: Dungeon Keeper 2
similar_games.why_similar: A classic indirect-control strategy game focused on managing supernatural minions, room-based powers, and tactical use of abilities rather than direct action combat.

similar_games.title: Evil Genius 2: World Domination
similar_games.why_similar: Scenario-driven lair management where success comes from trap placement, staff specialization, and setting up systems that manipulate intruders indirectly.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player horror-themed strategy/adventure game centered on commanding specters, solving puzzles, and resource management across distinct locations. However, the available text does not cleanly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The gameplay reads closest to a god-game / tactical puzzle / strategy hybrid, which is outside the permitted archetype list. While there are strong signals for horror setting, tactical optimization, and unit control, key identity facets needed to force-fit an allowed archetype are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective, no clear RTS base-building/economy loop, no turn-based tactics framing, no shooter/action framing, and combat itself appears indirect rather than a dominant conventional combat loop. Given the apparent taxonomy gap and lack of a strong allowable match, rejection is the safest outcome.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The original game this remake is based on; same core loop of placing specters, chaining haunt abilities, and solving scenario objectives through indirect control.

similar_games.why_similar: A more technical 2D beat 'em up emphasizing cancels, branching combo strings, throws, and mastery-heavy crowd control within classic stage-based brawling.

similar_games.why_similar: Classic arcade-style co-op beat 'em up with urban levels, weapon pickups, throws, and a straightforward but skill-based hand-to-hand combat loop.

similar_games.title: River City Girls 2
similar_games.why_similar: Co-op side-scrolling brawler with character-specific move sets, chaining attacks into longer combos, and a more expanded progression structure layered onto the core beat-'em-up loop.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify The Coin Game as an open-world collection of ticket-redemption arcade activities with realistic physics, carnival attractions, laser tag, mini-golf, karting, prize redemption, scavenging for money, and light survival-style needs management. The evidence is strong about the game's premise, but it does not cleanly align with any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. Top computed candidates like sports_sim, pinball, and party_game are partial fits at best and fail to capture the product's core identity as an open-world arcade/minigame sandbox. Because the available archetype set lacks a good match and forcing one would misclassify the gameplay identity, rejection is the safest outcome.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam descriptions consistently identify this as a basketball game centered on 5v5 matches, local multiplayer PvP, and a 1-player story mode. The core loop is explicitly match-based sports competition: '5 on 5 Pro Basketball,' 'side-scrolling, sweaty arcade action,' 'simplified 3-button gameplay,' 'dynamic play-calling,' large rosters with individualized attributes, and local multiplayer. While it has retro arcade presentation and a light story/unlock structure, the dominant identity is still a sports game built around winning basketball matches rather than a general party format. Secondary party-game adjacency is supported by local multiplayer and casual pickup-and-play framing, but sports_sim is the clearer fit.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Arcade-first basketball with simple inputs, fast match structure, and strong couch-multiplayer appeal.

Metacritic explicitly labels it a '3D Beat-'Em-Up,' which aligns with the core loop of third-person melee crowd-clearing combat across staged battles. The game also includes officer progression and tactical battlefield framing, supporting a light secondary resemblance to character action, but the dominant identity is musou/beat-'em-up style melee crowd control.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: It is the original game this remaster is built from, with the same stage-based Three Kingdoms musou structure, officers, and core 1-vs-1,000 combat.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crimson Desert as a third-person open-world action-adventure/RPG set in a medieval fantasy world, with free exploration across wilderness, cities, ruins, and the Abyss. Core play includes questing, battles, challenging enemies and bosses, multiple weapons and skills, elemental enhancements, dodges/counters/grapples, and traversal via climbing, gliding, and horseback. Progression includes character growth, skill development, equipment, and rebuilding/upgrading the Greymane camp, supporting an action-RPG identity over pure action-adventure. The strongest fit is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, with secondary overlap into open_world_action_adventure and some soulslike signals from punishing boss-focused combat.

That establishes a clear gameplay identity, but it does not map cleanly to any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not supported as pinball, racer, sports, card battler, management tycoon, or other listed archetypes, and forcing a fit would misclassify the title. Rejecting is appropriate because the game appears to sit outside the current archetype set rather than lacking evidence.

title: DigDigDrill
anchor_summary: A single-player underground digging and loot-grind game built around descending floors, filling your bag, returning to the surface, and forging stronger drills with synergistic effects. Its clearest neighbors are mining-centric progression games with strong upgrade loops, build crafting, and repeated delve-and-return structure rather than pure combat action.

similar_games.title: SteamWorld Dig 2
similar_games.why_similar: Side-view digging, ore collection, return-and-upgrade progression, and steadily stronger traversal tools make it the most immediate mining-focused comp.

similar_games.title: Dome Keeper
similar_games.why_similar: Centered on mining underground for resources and converting each haul into meaningful upgrades, with a very similar dig-surface-improve loop.

similar_games.title: Wall World
similar_games.why_similar: Combines fast mining runs, bag-filling resource extraction, and build-focused equipment upgrades in a compact repeated descent structure.

similar_games.title: Core Keeper
similar_games.why_similar: A broader sandbox, but still heavily driven by mining, ore collection, gear crafting, and long-term power growth through underground exploration.

similar_games.title: Mr. DRILLER DrillLand
similar_games.why_similar: Another drill-and-descend game focused on carving downward through blocks, though it is more arcade and less loot-build driven.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present DigDigDrill as a single-player underground mining and resource-collection game focused on digging for minerals and treasure, returning to the surface when inventory is full, and forging/customizing drills through a puzzle-like smithing/build-synergy system. The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. The dominant loop appears to be relaxed resource gathering, build crafting, and progression through deeper floors, but the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clear non-combat mining / digging / crafting progression archetype that this cleanly fits. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Side-view digging, ore collection, return-and-upgrade progression, and steadily stronger traversal tools make it the most immediate mining-focused comp.

anchor_summary: Lane-based tower defense clearly modeled on Plants vs. Zombies: place unit types to stop waves across many campaign stages, earn currency, and unlock/purchase support powers and stronger defenders.

similar_games.title: A Monster's Expedition

similar_games.title: Tametsi
similar_games.why_similar: A pure deduction puzzle game focused on uncovering patterns from clear rules, appealing to players who want tougher logic mastery.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports a non-combat, cozy single-player handcrafted puzzle game focused on coloring stained glass, rule-based logic puzzles, secret optional puzzles, and a level editor. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not include a general logic/puzzle-game bucket. The system-generated near miss for hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the actual description, which emphasizes rule-based handcrafted puzzles rather than object-finding scenes. Because the strongest evidence indicates a puzzle identity that does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.
